subject: Adding models-pap module to policy/models
commit_message:
Adding models-pap module to policy/models

1) Adding models-pap module to policy/models for storing models required
by PAP REST API's.
2) Adding test cases.

Change-Id: I4e8c38f1ebe96e7a50a188f19f4f4a5bed835f99
Issue-ID: POLICY-1541
Signed-off-by: ramverma <ram.krishna.verma@est.tech>

Yes

Patch Set 3:

(1 comment)

We will probably need to restructure this model to get it JPA complinat with keys etc. Also we will need to add unit test to test the JPA functionality works towards the H2 database.

Patch Set 3:

Do you want that done now? Or should we bring it in to further development?

Patch Set 2:

Does the minInstanceCount relate in any way to Kubernetes replica count?   If it is, I don't think it can/should be kept in sync.  I am not sure about other deployment info (as it seems kubernetes related info).  I have an issue in general accessing kubernetes control information from our containers.  I can approve the review, but I am concerned with having fields defined in the API that may not serve a clear purpose (and will left empty).  Let me know if you want me to approve the review as is and deal later with those fields.

Patch Set 3:

(1 comment)

> Does the minInstanceCount relate in any way to Kubernetes replica
 > count?   If it is, I don't think it can/should be kept in sync.  I
 > am not sure about other deployment info (as it seems kubernetes
 > related info).  I have an issue in general accessing kubernetes
 > control information from our containers.  I can approve the review,
 > but I am concerned with having fields defined in the API that may
 > not serve a clear purpose (and will left empty).  Let me know if
 > you want me to approve the review as is and deal later with those
 > fields.

Yes minInstanceCount relate to Kubernetes replica count. If we remove this field then how can a Policy Administrator inform PAP that I want to run these many instances of a PDP for this particular policy. I mean how do we achieve the auto scalability that we thought of achieving using PDPSubgroup & Kubernetes.

Patch Set 3:

I think we have to keep some information in the PAP on what we want K8S to do. How all this works towards K8S might not be firmed up yet but I think we should leave it in there for now.

Patch Set 3:

I have some ideas on how we may get this to work:

I think we can safely say there are 2 levels of scaling: (1) at the kubernetes cluster level, (2) at the application level.

(2) cannot control anything from (1).    A necessary precondition for (2) is that independently a k8s cluster admin scales out the appropriate pdp pods (1).

A change in (1) does not propagate to (2), there are different abstractions.   But an operation in (1) have sideeffects that (2) can use for inferring the dynamic size of pooled resources.

Probably the minInstanceCount can be kept or perhaps renamed to desiredInstanceCount,
It is though probably think of it as an application level concept independent of kubernetes, although there is an indirect relationship due to the side effects I mentioned earlier.

kubernetes admin can and will change replica counts as they wish using their tooling, kubectl, helm, gui or others, as it is just a simple command operation that changes the replica count for a deployment.   This information does not flow directly to the application.   That's what I meant is difficult to keep in sync, any changes they introduce in the replica count with the application running in the containers.

But this will translate at runtime as the side effect I mentioned earlier with new PDPs registering (for scaling) or just stopping sending pdp-status notifications (de-scaling) so effectively you know what's the size of your pool of pdps at any given time.   The size of the pool can be very dynamic, and cannot be controlled by the application.   Based on that, PAP can decide how to allocate to groups and provide autoscalability within autoscalability, perhpas based on this minInstanceCount.   Point being is that the "min" in "minInstanceCount" is somewhat out of our application control.

Thoughts?

Patch Set 3:

> I have some ideas on how we may get this to work:
 > 
 > I think we can safely say there are 2 levels of scaling: (1) at the
 > kubernetes cluster level, (2) at the application level.
 > 
 > (2) cannot control anything from (1).    A necessary precondition
 > for (2) is that independently a k8s cluster admin scales out the
 > appropriate pdp pods (1).
 > 
 > A change in (1) does not propagate to (2), there are different
 > abstractions.   But an operation in (1) have sideeffects that (2)
 > can use for inferring the dynamic size of pooled resources.
 > 
 > Probably the minInstanceCount can be kept or perhaps renamed to
 > desiredInstanceCount,
 > It is though probably think of it as an application level concept
 > independent of kubernetes, although there is an indirect
 > relationship due to the side effects I mentioned earlier.
 > 
 > kubernetes admin can and will change replica counts as they wish
 > using their tooling, kubectl, helm, gui or others, as it is just a
 > simple command operation that changes the replica count for a
 > deployment.   This information does not flow directly to the
 > application.   That's what I meant is difficult to keep in sync,
 > any changes they introduce in the replica count with the
 > application running in the containers.
 > 
 > But this will translate at runtime as the side effect I mentioned
 > earlier with new PDPs registering (for scaling) or just stopping
 > sending pdp-status notifications (de-scaling) so effectively you
 > know what's the size of your pool of pdps at any given time.   The
 > size of the pool can be very dynamic, and cannot be controlled by
 > the application.   Based on that, PAP can decide how to allocate to
 > groups and provide autoscalability within autoscalability, perhpas
 > based on this minInstanceCount.   Point being is that the "min" in
 > "minInstanceCount" is somewhat out of our application control.
 > 
 > Thoughts?

That's what I was wondering. Can a new instance find out unique details about itself and pass that to the PAP? So the PAP can uniquely identify new ones and then safely get rid of stale instances.

I don't think the pap should be scaling anything, but at least ensuring that new PDP's go into a group and are able to synch policies etc.

 > That's what I was wondering. Can a new instance find out unique

 > instances.
 > 
 > I don't think the pap should be scaling anything, but at least
 > ensuring that new PDP's go into a group and are able to synch
 > policies etc.

The pod's name is the hostname.  I would assume the instanceId in this message is this hostname I mentioned (which is the pod name).

I haven't seen other uses other than that just the typical static configuration passed in the config maps, secrets, etc .. at initialization.

But a PAP monitoring the pdp-status and tracking registrations can make a good approximation on the active set of PDPs and decide on group assignment according to some strategy.

Patch Set 3: Code-Review+1

Approving to avoid blocking folks, we should continue conversation though.
